text: 72g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for nuts, fat quality has to justify the calorie density.
13.9g of carbs. fibre is doing a lot of the work here. for a nut, you need enough upside to make the density worth it.
9.2g of protein. bioavailability is one of the best parts of the protein story. protein is support here, not the whole argument.
vitamin e at 10% daily value stands out most. that is barely moving the needle.
magnesium at 34% daily value, zinc at 29% daily value, iron at 14% daily value stand out most. useful, but still not a major mineral edge.

the upsides first: fat and texture help satiety. they can hold up better than many snack foods. holds up as a better snack default than most packaged options. that real-world swap value counts. texture helps them feel satisfying. that can make moderation easier than with softer snack foods.

on the weaker side: cost or allergy issues can limit use. practical downsides still count. good versions can get expensive. that affects how realistic frequent use is. portion control can get slippery fast. dense foods punish casual handful math.
B tier. Best in small amounts where the fats and minerals matter more than sheer calories.
